# Key Figures

Topics covering notable individuals who shaped the year 1776.

<!-- BEGIN GENERATED: category-index — maintained by build-structure; do not edit by hand -->

| Topic | Description |
|---|---|
| [Thomas Paine](thomas-paine.md) | English-born author of *Common Sense* (January 10, 1776), the pamphlet that galvanized colonial support for independence and reached an estimated 75,000–500,000 readers across the colonies and Europe. |
| [Joseph Brant (Thayendanegea) in 1776](joseph-brant.md) | Mohawk leader and diplomat whose 1775–76 London mission secured a British alliance for the Mohawk Nation, fractured the Iroquois Confederacy, and shaped the Revolutionary War's frontier theater. |
| [John Adams & the 1776 Continental Congress](john-adams-1776.md) | Massachusetts delegate and leading advocate for independence, who served on the Committee of Five and championed the Declaration's adoption while chairing the Board of War. |
| [Thomas Jefferson in 1776](thomas-jefferson-1776.md) | Virginia delegate and principal author of the Declaration of Independence, who drafted it in June 1776 and afterward began reforming Virginia's laws in the new House of Delegates. |
| [Nathan Hale: The Continental Army's Martyr Spy](nathan-hale-1776.md) | Yale-educated Continental officer and member of Knowlton's Rangers, hanged by the British as a spy on September 22, 1776, whose defiant last words made him a founding American martyr. |
| [Benjamin Franklin & 1776 Diplomacy](benjamin-franklin-1776.md) | Statesman and natural philosopher who, at 70, served on the Committee of Five, undertook the failed Canada mission, and sailed to France in October 1776 to secure the alliance critical to American independence. |
| [Lord George Germain: Britain's War Minister](lord-george-germain-1776.md) | Secretary of State for the Colonies from 1775 to 1782 and Britain's chief strategist for suppressing the American Revolution, whose coordination of the 1776 campaigns and later miscalculations contributed to British defeat. |
| [George Washington in 1776](george-washington-1776.md) | Commander-in-chief of the Continental Army, who liberated Boston in March 1776, lost New York through a summer and autumn of defeats, and recovered with the Delaware crossing and victory at Trenton on December 26, 1776. |

<!-- END GENERATED: category-index -->
